frontier_title: Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system
trajectory_ascii:
Discovering mathematical concepts t... (2026)
├── Lean Copilot: Large Language Models... (2024)
│   ├── Evaluating language models for math... (2023)  (background)
│   ├── A Survey on Hallucination in Large ... (2023)  (background)
│   ├── Machine-Learned Premise Selection f... (2023)  (background)
│   ├── FIMO: A Challenge Formal Dataset fo... (2023)  (background)
│   ├── Magnushammer: A Transformer-based A... (2023)  (background)
│   ├── LeanDojo: Theorem Proving with Retr... (2023)
│   └── LLMSTEP: LLM proofstep suggestions ... (2023)
├── Machine learning and information th... (2024)
│   └── Symbols and mental programs: a hypo... (2022)
├── The Equational Theories Project: Ad... (2025)
└── AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for sci... (2025)  (background)
trajectory_description: The main research lines are (1) integrating large language models with formal theorem provers (Lean Copilot, LeanDojo, LLMSTEP) and addressing challenges like hallucination and premise selection, (2) exploring theoretical foundations for an AI mathematician through machine learning and information theory, and (3) scaling collaborative mathematical research via automated equational reasoning (The Equational Theories Project) and coding agents for scientific discovery (AlphaEvolve). These lines converge in the frontier paper, "Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system," which combines multi-agent architectures with LLM-based reasoning and formal proof environments to autonomously generate and validate new mathematical knowledge. Overall, the evolution traces a trajectory from isolated advances in AI-assisted theorem proving and conceptual foundations to integrated, scalable systems that leverage collaboration and automation to discover novel mathematics.

f5fb1fc7b2c4a5382f5adc291bc1e89f76ea2f5c.title: Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system

entities.name: Multi-agent system
entities.description: Computational model with multiple agents that pose conjectures and attempt proofs

entities.name: Euler's conjecture for polyhedra
entities.description: Historical mathematical problem inspiring the benchmark

entities.name: Homology concept
entities.description: Target concept to be autonomously recovered from polyhedral data

entities.name: Polyhedral data
entities.description: Input data representing geometric polyhedra

entities.name: Linear algebra
entities.description: Knowledge domain provided to the system

idea.background: Mathematical concepts emerge through experimentation, proof, and counterexamples. Prior work in computational discovery often lacks dynamic feedback loops. An open challenge in the literature involves autonomously recovering homology from polyhedral data.
idea.novelty: The paper introduces a multi-agent model that generates its own conjectures, attempts proofs, and adapts based on feedback and an evolving data distribution. This dynamic process is inspired by the history of Euler's conjecture and is benchmarked on recovering homology, demonstrating the value of combining local processes.
idea.contribution: The primary method is a multi-agent system where agents propose conjectures, attempt proofs, and update a shared data distribution based on outcomes. Key components include conjecture generation, proof attempts, and feedback-driven adaptation. The system successfully recovers homology and ablation experiments statistically validate the complete dynamic.
idea.method: The system is implemented with agents that independently pose conjectures using an initial data distribution. These conjectures are attempted to be proven using linear algebra rules. Successes and failures update the data distribution, influencing future conjectures. The benchmark task involves recovering homology from polyhedral data. The architecture is a multi-agent reinforcement learning setup where each agent's policy is learned from the evolving data. Training uses a curriculum of increasing problem complexity. Key design choices include decentralized conjecture generation and centralized proof verification.
idea.detail_reason: The combination of decentralized exploration (conjecture posing) and centralized verification (proof attempts) allows the system to efficiently search the hypothesis space. The feedback-driven data distribution naturally focuses on promising directions, mimicking the historical process of mathematical discovery.
idea.limitation: The system is evaluated only on a specific benchmark (homology recovery) and may not generalize to other mathematical domains. It assumes access to a formal proof system and linear algebra background. The ablation studies suggest component importance but may not capture all interactions.

experiment.process: Ablation studies comparing the full dynamic system against variants where components (e.g., feedback, data distribution update) are turned off. Each variant is tested on the homology recovery task.
experiment.technical_details: Implemented using a custom multi-agent framework with reinforcement learning. Data consists of polyhedral graphs. Proof attempts use automated theorem proving with linear algebra. The system is trained from scratch.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include static data distribution, no feedback, and random conjecture generation. Comparisons control for experimental setup.
experiment.verification: The experiments show that the full dynamic system significantly outperforms baselines in recovering homology, supporting that the interplay of local processes leads to better notions of mathematical interestingness.

references: Euler's conjecture for polyhedra and its historical resolution
Computational discovery of mathematical concepts using multi-agent systems
Homology theory and its applications in algebraic topology

evolution_steps.from_title: Lean Copilot: Large Language Models as Copilots for Theorem Proving in Lean

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's LLM-based copilot only suggests proof steps for human-given goals, lacking autonomy to propose new conjectures or explore mathematical concepts.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A replaces the single LLM with a multi-agent system where agents autonomously generate conjectures, attempt proofs using linear algebra rules, and update a shared data distribution based on outcomes.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B uses a single LLM to assist humans with proof steps; A uses decentralized agents that collectively discover concepts via conjecture-proving feedback loops, removing the need for human-provided goals.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A's multi-agent system is evaluated only on homology recovery and does not incorporate human feedback or broad mathematical knowledge, leaving the challenge of complex, human-guided theorem proving untouched.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A still assumes access to a formal proof system and predefined inference rules, similar to B's reliance on a proof assistant's environment.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's LLM-based copilot only suggests proof steps for human-given goals, lacking autonomy to propose new conjectures or explore mathematical concepts.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B uses a single LLM to assist humans with proof steps; A uses decentralized agents that collectively discover concepts via conjecture-proving feedback loops, removing the need for human-provided goals.

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's lemma generation is centralized in a single LLM, leading to limited diversity in candidate lemmas and potential bias in exploration.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A uses multiple agents that independently propose conjectures based on a shared but evolving data distribution, increasing diversity and enabling feedback-driven adaptation.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces B's single LLM-based lemma generator with a decentralized multi-agent conjecture generation and proof attempt system.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A retains B's reliance on a predefined formal system (linear algebra rules) and evaluates only on a single benchmark (homology recovery), leaving the generalizability gap from B's IMO-specific focus unaddressed.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption that a fixed formal system (e.g., Lean, linear algebra) is available for proof verification, constraining the approach to domains where such a system exists.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's lemma generation is centralized in a single LLM, leading to limited diversity in candidate lemmas and potential bias in exploration.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A replaces B's single LLM-based lemma generator with a decentralized multi-agent conjecture generation and proof attempt system.

evolution_steps.from_title: The Equational Theories Project: Advancing Collaborative Mathematical Research at Scale

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B lacks an automated mechanism for generating new conjectures; it relies on a fixed set of predefined equational laws and does not learn from proof outcomes.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces a multi-agent system where agents propose conjectures based on a dynamically updated data distribution, and proof attempts (success/failure) update that distribution, enabling automated discovery.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces B's static conjecture set with a feedback-driven conjecture generation loop, where proof outcomes guide future conjectures.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B only explored the 4694 simplest equational laws on magmas; A similarly restricts evaluation to a single benchmark (homology recovery) and does not demonstrate generalization to other mathematical domains or more complex laws.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A assumes access to a domain-specific formal proof system (linear algebra rules) similar to B's dependence on Lean for verification.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B lacks an automated mechanism for generating new conjectures; it relies on a fixed set of predefined equational laws and does not learn from proof outcomes.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A replaces B's static conjecture set with a feedback-driven conjecture generation loop, where proof outcomes guide future conjectures.

evolution_steps.from_title: Machine learning and information theory concepts towards an AI Mathematician

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B is a purely conceptual framework with no concrete algorithm for conjecture generation, proof attempts, or adaptation; it lacks implementation and experimental validation.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A implements a multi-agent system where agents generate conjectures from a data distribution, attempt proofs using linear algebra, and update the data distribution based on success/failure, enabling the system to learn mathematical concepts.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: From a conceptual information-theoretic objective to a concrete multi-agent reinforcement learning system with decentralized conjecture generation and centralized proof verification.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A's approach is evaluated only on a specific homology recovery benchmark and assumes a formal proof system (linear algebra) is available; it remains unclear whether the method generalizes to other mathematical domains where such formalization is not straightforward.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption that the target mathematical domain can be quantified via derivation steps (here, linear algebra rules), without addressing the challenge of automatically constructing such formal systems for arbitrary domains.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B is a purely conceptual framework with no concrete algorithm for conjecture generation, proof attempts, or adaptation; it lacks implementation and experimental validation.

what_changed.mechanism_change: From a conceptual information-theoretic objective to a concrete multi-agent reinforcement learning system with decentralized conjecture generation and centralized proof verification.

evolution_steps.from_title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's evolutionary algorithm relies on predefined evaluators (e.g., test cases) to judge code mutations, but for open-ended mathematical discovery, there is no straightforward automated evaluator for conjectures or proofs.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A replaces the code mutation and evaluator with a multi-agent system where agents propose conjectures, attempt proofs using a formal system (linear algebra rules), and update a shared data distribution based on proof outcomes.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B evolves code via mutations evaluated by external tests; A evolves a data distribution via agents proposing conjectures verified by a formal proof system.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: Both A and B require a domain-specific, reliable evaluation mechanism (B: test cases; A: formal proof system) and do not provide a way to learn or generalize evaluation to domains without such mechanisms.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A retains the assumption that a formal verification system exists for the target domain, analogous to B's assumption of reliable task evaluators.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's evolutionary algorithm relies on predefined evaluators (e.g., test cases) to judge code mutations, but for open-ended mathematical discovery, there is no straightforward automated evaluator for conjectures or proofs.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B evolves code via mutations evaluated by external tests; A evolves a data distribution via agents proposing conjectures verified by a formal proof system.

evolution_steps.from_title: What makes math problems hard for reinforcement learning: a case study

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's RL approach only searches over existing group presentations defined by user; it cannot generate new mathematical conjectures autonomously.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces a multi-agent system where agents independently propose conjectures using an evolving data distribution, thereby generating new mathematical statements to prove.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B uses a single-agent RL to navigate the space of presentations, while A uses multiple agents that generate conjectures and update a shared data distribution based on proof outcomes.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A still only works on a constrained mathematical domain (homology recovery) and does not generalize to other mathematical concepts, similar to B's limitation to group presentations.
gap_analysis.what_persists: Both assume a formal, deterministic environment with exact proof rules and require domain-specific background knowledge (linear algebra for A, group theory for B).

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's RL approach only searches over existing group presentations defined by user; it cannot generate new mathematical conjectures autonomously.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B uses a single-agent RL to navigate the space of presentations, while A uses multiple agents that generate conjectures and update a shared data distribution based on proof outcomes.

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's single-agent lemma-style proof generation explores hypotheses along a single chain, lacking distributed parallel conjecture generation and selection.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces a multi-agent system where agents independently propose conjectures, attempt proofs using linear algebra, and update a shared data distribution based on outcomes, enabling decentralized exploration.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces centralized single-agent proof generation with decentralized multi-agent conjecture-propose-verify loop.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A still relies on a formal proof system (linear algebra rules) and is only evaluated on a specific benchmark (homology recovery), not addressing generalization to informal mathematical reasoning or other domains.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption of a formal verification environment and centralized proof verification, limiting applicability to domains with formalized rules.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's single-agent lemma-style proof generation explores hypotheses along a single chain, lacking distributed parallel conjecture generation and selection.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A replaces centralized single-agent proof generation with decentralized multi-agent conjecture-propose-verify loop.

evolution_steps.from_title: Olympiad-level formal mathematical reasoning with reinforcement learning

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's single-agent AlphaProof uses a centralized neural network to guide tree search in a formal environment, which limits exploration of diverse conjectures and requires extensive auto-formalization for each problem.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces a multi-agent system with decentralized conjecture generation where agents independently propose conjectures based on a shared data distribution, and centralized proof verification provides feedback to update the distribution, enabling collaborative discovery.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: Single-agent RL with tree search (B) vs. multi-agent RL with shared data distribution and decentralized conjecture generation (A).

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A still evaluates only on a single formal benchmark (homology recovery) and does not address generalization to other mathematical domains or geometry, mirroring B's limited success on IMO geometry problems.
gap_analysis.what_persists: Both methods depend on a predefined formal environment (Lean for B, linear algebra rules for A) and do not incorporate reasoning in informal or mixed mathematical contexts.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's single-agent AlphaProof uses a centralized neural network to guide tree search in a formal environment, which limits exploration of diverse conjectures and requires extensive auto-formalization for each problem.

what_changed.mechanism_change: Single-agent RL with tree search (B) vs. multi-agent RL with shared data distribution and decentralized conjecture generation (A).
